section.title: Paul before Felix
section.sectionRationale: The riot and the plot of the previous chapters give way to the first of Paul's formal hearings before Roman power. A hired orator prosecutes; Paul answers point by point; and the governor, gripped and yet unwilling, leaves him in prison for two years. The gospel is now on trial before the empire, exactly as the risen Christ had promised.
section.thesis: Accused of sedition and sacrilege, Paul calmly answers that the true charge against him is the hope of the resurrection; and when he reasons of righteousness, self-control, and coming judgment, it is the judge who trembles, while a clear conscience keeps the prisoner free.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Tertullus prosecutes Paul before Felix; Paul makes his measured defense; the real issue surfaces as the resurrection of the dead; and Felix, alarmed yet greedy, postpones the verdict and leaves Paul bound.

verses.text: Five days later the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and a lawyer named Tertullus, who presented to the governor their case against Paul.
When Paul had been called in, Tertullus opened the prosecution: “Because of you, we have enjoyed a lasting peace, and your foresight has brought improvements to this nation.
In every way and everywhere, most excellent Felix, we acknowledge this with all gratitude.
But in order not to delay you any further, I beg your indulgence to hear us briefly.
We have found this man to be a pestilence, stirring up dissension among the Jews all over the world. He is a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes,
and he even tried to desecrate the temple; so we seized him.
By examining him yourself, you will be able to learn the truth about all our charges against him.”
The Jews concurred, asserting that these charges were true.

units.label: Paul makes his defense
units.note: worshiping God according to the Way

verses.text: When the governor motioned for Paul to speak, he began his response: “Knowing that you have been a judge over this nation for many years, I gladly make my defense.
You can verify for yourself that no more than twelve days ago I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
Yet my accusers did not find me debating with anyone in the temple or riling up a crowd in the synagogues or in the city.
Nor can they prove to you any of their charges against me.

I do confess to you, however, that I worship the God of our fathers according to the {{hodos|Way}}, which they call a sect. I believe everything that is laid down by the Law and written in the Prophets,
and I have the same hope in God that they themselves cherish, that there will be a {{anastasis|resurrection}} of both the righteous and the wicked.
In this hope, I strive always to maintain a clear {{syneidesis|conscience}} before God and man.

units.label: The real issue: the resurrection
units.note: on trial for the hope of the dead raised

verses.text: After several years, then, I returned to Jerusalem to bring alms to my people and to present offerings.
At the time they found me in the temple, I was ceremonially clean and was not inciting a crowd or an uproar. But there are some Jews from the province of Asia
who ought to appear before you and bring charges, if they have anything against me.
Otherwise, let these men state for themselves any crime they found in me when I stood before the Sanhedrin,
unless it was this one thing I called out as I stood in their presence: ‘It is concerning the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today.’”

units.label: Felix trembles and delays
units.note: righteousness, self-control, and the coming judgment

verses.text: Then Felix, who was well informed about the Way, adjourned the hearing and said, “When Lysias the commander comes, I will decide your case.”
He ordered the centurion to keep Paul under guard, but to allow him some freedom and permit his friends to minister to his needs.
After several days, Felix returned with his wife Drusilla, who was a Jewess. He sent for Paul and listened to him speak about faith in Christ Jesus.
As Paul expounded on {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}}, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix became frightened and said, “You may go for now. When I find the time, I will call for you.”
At the same time, he was hoping that Paul would offer him a bribe. So he sent for Paul frequently and talked with him.
After two years had passed, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus. And wishing to do the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul in prison.

hodos.gloss: A road or way. The earliest name for the Christian movement was simply the Way, Acts 9:2, echoing Jesus who called Himself the way, John 14:6. Paul insists this Way is not a novel sect but the true worship of the God of the fathers, the fulfillment of the Law and the Prophets.

anastasis.gloss: A rising up, resurrection. Paul stakes his whole defense on the hope of resurrection, the raising of both righteous and wicked. In Acts the resurrection is never an abstract doctrine but rests on the raising of Jesus, the firstfruits, which is why this hope keeps landing Paul in court.

syneidesis.gloss: Conscience, the inner knowing that judges one's own conduct. Paul labors to keep a clear conscience before God and man. It is not a claim of sinless perfection but of integrity: he has hidden nothing and wronged no one, so the accusations cannot stick.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, what is just and right. Paul reasons with Felix about righteousness, self-control, and the judgment to come, pressing the gospel on the conscience of a corrupt and immoral judge. The word that should have humbled Felix instead frightened him, and he sent Paul away.

basic.context: Paul has been rescued from a mob and a murder plot and moved to Caesarea, the seat of Roman government. Now the high priest brings a lawyer, Tertullus, to prosecute him before the governor Felix. The charges are political and religious: Paul is a troublemaker, a ringleader of the Nazarenes, and a temple-defiler. Paul answers each charge and points to the one real issue behind them all.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as a courtroom scene with three speeches: the prosecution, the defense, and a private conversation. Notice how Luke sets flattering, unproven accusation against calm, verifiable fact. The turning point is Paul's own admission in verses 14 to 21: the thing they cannot forgive is his hope in the resurrection.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul worships God according to the Way, the hodos, the earliest name for the church. His hope is the anastasis, the resurrection of the dead. He keeps a clear syneidesis, conscience, before God and man. And when he reasons with Felix about dikaiosyne, righteousness, self-control, and judgment, the governor is shaken.
basic.typology: Paul on trial before a Roman governor, innocent yet accused, echoes his Lord, who also stood before a governor on false charges of sedition and temple offense. The servant follows the pattern of the Master, and the false witnesses cannot make their case stand.
basic.christ: The unspoken center of the trial is Jesus. The resurrection Paul defends is grounded in the raising of Christ. The Way he confesses is the way of Christ. And when he speaks with Felix about faith in Christ Jesus, and about righteousness and coming judgment, he is preaching the risen Judge to the man who holds his fate.
basic.god: God governs even a rigged trial. Paul is protected, given freedom, and allowed to keep preaching. The God of the fathers whom Paul worships is the same God who will one day judge Felix and every ruler. His providence keeps His servant safe until the appointed testimony in Rome is given.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 23 sets up the plot and transfer to Caesarea. Luke 23 shows Jesus before Pilate on similar charges. Acts 24:15 rests on 1 Corinthians 15, the resurrection of the dead. Acts 24:25 recalls Jesus' warning that we must give account at the judgment.
basic.oneStory: The risen Christ told Paul he must testify in Rome. This trial is one step on that road: the gospel carried up the ladder of Roman authority, from mob to governor, on its way to Caesar and the ends of the earth. Even Paul's imprisonment serves the advance of the word.

context.literary: This is the first of three set-piece defenses that close the book of Acts, before Felix, Festus, and Agrippa. Luke structures it as a formal trial: a hired orator's flattering prosecution, Paul's orderly rebuttal, and a private sequel with the governor. The repeated legal hearings let Luke show, again and again, that the gospel is charged but never convicted.
context.historical: Antonius Felix was procurator of Judea around AD 52 to 59, a former slave notorious for cruelty and corruption. His wife Drusilla was a Jewish princess he had lured from another marriage. Caesarea was the Roman capital of the province. Tertullus opens with the customary flattery of a Roman patron, then lays out charges designed to sound like sedition, the one thing Rome would punish.
context.canonical: The trial narratives fulfill Jesus' word that His witnesses would be dragged before governors and kings for His name's sake, Luke 21:12 to 13, and the risen Lord's promise that Paul would testify in Rome, Acts 23:11. They also show the pattern of the innocent sufferer whose accusers cannot prove their charges.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the charges against Paul, and so against the Christian movement, are baseless, that the true issue is the resurrection hope, and that even a corrupt governor is confronted by the claims of God through the gospel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a particular Roman trial; it does not prescribe its details. The abiding truths are the innocence of the gospel before every honest court, the centrality of the resurrection, and the way the word of God presses conscience even in the powerful.

debates.question: Why did Felix keep Paul in prison for two years?

views.label: To do the Jews a favor
views.case: Luke states plainly that Felix, wishing to do the Jews a favor, left Paul bound, Acts 24:27; a weak and self-serving governor avoids offending the accusers rather than releasing an innocent man.

views.label: In hope of a bribe
views.case: Luke also says Felix hoped Paul would offer him money, verse 26, so greed kept the case open; the two motives combine, political cowardice and corruption, in a man who was frightened by the truth yet ruled by self-interest.

debates.bearing: Either way the delay is a moral failure, not a legal one. Felix has heard the gospel, felt its force, and chosen convenience over conversion; the text warns against the deadly habit of putting off God's call to a more convenient time.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of Paul's defense turns on a handful of terms. He worships according to the hodos, the Way, the earliest self-description of the church and no mere sect. His hope is the anastasis, the resurrection of the dead, which he says is the real charge against him. He keeps a clear syneidesis, conscience, before God and man, claiming integrity rather than perfection. And in private he reasons with Felix about dikaiosyne, righteousness, together with self-control and judgment to come, so that the gospel indicts the judge.

typology.fulfillment: Foreshadowed by Christ before Pilate, the innocent servant accused of sedition and temple offense whose charges cannot be proven, Luke 23:1 to 4.

typology.type: The resurrection of the just and the unjust
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and grounded in the risen Christ, the firstfruits, who guarantees the resurrection Paul defends, 1 Corinthians 15:20 to 23.

typology.type: The judge who trembles yet delays
typology.fulfillment: A picture of every hearer who feels the truth of coming judgment but puts off repentance; answered only by turning to Christ before the day arrives, Hebrews 3:15.

study.christ: Jesus is the silent center of the whole trial. The Way Paul confesses is the way of Christ, John 14:6. The resurrection he defends is secured by Christ's own rising. When Luke says Felix listened to Paul speak about faith in Christ Jesus, and Paul reasoned of righteousness and the judgment to come, we glimpse the sermon: the crucified and risen Lord is the coming Judge, and faith in Him is the only refuge. The prisoner preaches Christ to the man who holds the keys.
study.god: God is sovereign over this courtroom. He had promised Paul would reach Rome, and He guards him through plot and trial, granting even a measure of freedom and access to friends. The God of the fathers whom Paul worships is the righteous Judge before whom Felix himself must one day stand. His patience keeps the door open to Felix for two years, yet His justice is not mocked by endless delay.

crossReferences.note: The risen Lord's promise that Paul must testify in Rome, the thread these trials serve.
Jesus before Pilate on charges of sedition and stirring up the people, the pattern Paul now follows.
The resurrection of the dead that Paul defends rests on Christ raised as the firstfruits.
The coming judgment Paul pressed on Felix: all must appear before the judgment seat of Christ.

study.oneStory: The ascended Christ is carrying His gospel from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, and the road runs through Roman courtrooms. Paul, bound but unbowed, testifies to the resurrection before a governor, and even in chains he preaches Christ and coming judgment to those in power. The word cannot be convicted or silenced; it presses on toward Caesar and Rome, just as the Lord had said.

quiz.q: According to Paul, what is the real reason he is on trial?

options.text: The hope of the resurrection of the dead.
options.note: Paul says plainly that the resurrection of the dead is the issue on which he is judged, verse 21.

options.text: His confession that he worships God according to the Way.
options.note: True and related, Paul does confess the Way, but he names the resurrection as the precise charge behind the hostility.

options.text: That he had actually defiled the temple.
options.note: This was the accusation, but Paul shows it cannot be proven; it is a false charge, not the real reason.

options.text: That he had led an armed revolt against Rome.
options.note: No such thing happened; the sedition charge is baseless and unproven.

quiz.q: How does Tertullus open the prosecution?

options.text: With flattery of Felix, then unproven charges that Paul is a seditious ringleader.
options.note: He praises Felix's rule, then accuses Paul of stirring dissension and defiling the temple, charges he cannot prove.

options.text: With a list of Paul's crimes backed by solid witnesses.
options.note: He does make charges, but Luke's point is that they are unsupported; there are no witnesses who can prove them.

options.text: With a fair and neutral summary of the facts.
options.note: The speech is neither fair nor neutral; it begins with calculated flattery and rhetorical accusation.

options.text: By admitting Paul had done nothing wrong.
options.note: The opposite; Tertullus calls Paul a pestilence and seeks his condemnation.

quiz.q: What happened when Paul reasoned with Felix about righteousness, self-control, and judgment?

options.text: Felix became frightened and sent Paul away for a more convenient time.
options.note: Luke says Felix was alarmed and postponed, verse 25, the tragedy of a man who feels the truth but delays.

options.text: Felix listened because he hoped Paul might offer him a bribe.
options.note: True of his ongoing motive, verse 26, but the immediate response to that sermon was fear and delay.

options.text: Felix repented and was baptized on the spot.
options.note: He was moved but did not repent; he put the decision off, which is the opposite of conversion.

options.text: Felix released Paul at once.
options.note: He did not release him; he left Paul in prison for two years.

quiz.q: Why did Felix leave Paul in prison for two years?

options.text: To do the Jews a favor and in hope of a bribe.
options.note: Luke gives both motives, verses 26 to 27: political cowardice and greed, not any proven guilt.

options.text: Because he wished to please the Jewish leaders.
options.note: This is one of the two stated reasons, but it leaves out his hope for money.

options.text: Because the evidence against Paul was strong.
options.note: The evidence was not strong; Paul was innocent, and the delay was a moral failure, not a legal necessity.

options.text: Because Paul had asked to remain in custody.
options.note: Paul made no such request; the imprisonment served Felix's interests, not Paul's.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by worshiping God according to the Way?

options.text: The Way is the true worship of the God of the fathers, fulfilling the Law and the Prophets.
options.note: Paul insists the Way is not a novel sect but the fulfillment of Israel's own Scriptures and hope.

options.text: The Way is the name the early church used for following Jesus.
options.note: True, this was the church's early self-description, but Paul's point here is its continuity with Israel's faith.

options.text: The Way is a breakaway sect that rejects the Old Testament.
options.note: Paul says the opposite; he believes everything in the Law and the Prophets.

options.text: The Way is a Roman religion approved by the state.
options.note: It was neither Roman nor state-approved; it was the faith of Israel fulfilled in Christ.

questions: Paul could face false accusation with a clear conscience because he had hidden nothing. Where in your life would a clear conscience before God and others bring freedom?
The resurrection hope was the thing Paul would not surrender, even in court. How much does the hope of resurrection actually shape the way you live and speak?
Felix was frightened by talk of righteousness, self-control, and judgment, yet he delayed. Where are you tempted to put off responding to God until a more convenient time that never comes?
Paul preached Christ to the very man who held him prisoner. Who has authority over you right now, and how might God be calling you to bear witness there?
God kept Paul safe and useful through two quiet years in prison. How do you respond when God's plan for you seems stalled or delayed?

translationNotes.note: The BSB omits verse 7, following the earliest and best Greek manuscripts, which move from verse 6 to verse 8. A footnote records that the later Received Text adds material at this point: and we would have judged him according to our law. But Lysias the commander came with great force and took him out of our hands, ordering his accusers to come before you. Because the addition is not part of the oldest text, the verse number is left unused. We carry the omission faithfully.

The Way translates the Greek hodos, road or way, the earliest name for the Christian movement, Acts 9:2. Paul uses it to stress that following Jesus is the true continuation of Israel's faith, not a breakaway sect.

Self-control renders a Greek term for mastery of the passions; pointed words to Felix, whose marriage to Drusilla and reputation for corruption made righteousness, self-control, and judgment cut close to home.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 24. Verse 24:7 is omitted by the BSB per the earliest manuscripts and is carried as an omission with a translation note. Footnote markers and inline note objects were skipped when building verse text; line breaks were treated as spaces. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
